label: fiction
title: The Choirmaster's Watch
charge: Burglary of the cathedral vestry and theft of its silver
elements: The vestry was broken into and the silver taken.

beats.text: The bloodhound's handler gives thrilling evidence: from the vestry sill the dog ran the scent across the market square, up the accused's lane, and bayed at his door.
beats.reveal_note: A two-day-old trail across a market square trodden by hundreds — and a handler who already knew whose door it was. The dog performed; it did not testify.

beats.text: The pawnbroker's ledger: the dean's watch, pledged under the name 'J. Marsh'. The handwriting examiner finds the ledger signature is the accused's verified hand.
beats.reveal_note: An alias in a man's own handwriting is a confession with a false name on it.

beats.text: A senior chorister speaks for the whole stalls: thirty years of unlocked doors and untouched collection boxes around this man. 'The idea is impossible.'
beats.reveal_note: Character is real evidence and real misdirection at once: it explains why nobody looked at him for thirty years — not what he did this winter.

beats.text: Found in the vice on the accused's workbench: silver filings. The assay matches the vestry plate — the same alloy the cathedral's Victorian silversmith recorded in the chapter books.
beats.reveal_note: Plate is awkward to sell whole. Filings in a vice are what turning sacred silver into anonymous metal looks like.

beats.text: The judge: the jury should follow the dog nowhere — such displays have misled better courts than this one. Weigh the ledger and the bench vice instead.
beats.reveal_note: Right verdict, right reasons: the theatrical evidence deserved nothing and the boring evidence deserved everything. Convict on the ledger, not the bloodhound.

twist: The bloodhound was theatre — a stale trail and a cued handler, worth nothing — and it bayed at a guilty man's door anyway. The pawn ledger in his own disguised hand and church silver filed to anonymity in his vice convict him without a single bark.
